concepts.title: The Arrhenius Concept
concepts.conceptBody:
1. The Arrhenius Concept:

Arrhenius classified a substance into an acid or a base in terms of the characteristics ions of water which it produces in aqueous solution. Thus an acid is a substance which ionizes in water to produce ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$(aq) or the hydronium ion; a base is a substance which produces $\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}(\text{aq})$

$\text{HCl}(\text{aq})\xrightarrow[{}]{}{{\text{H}}^{+}}(\text{aq})+\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}(\text{aq})\text{ }$

$\text{NaOH}(\text{aq})\xrightarrow{{}}\text{N}{{\text{a}}^{+}}(\text{aq})+\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}(\text{aq})$

  • The strength of an acid is defined in terms of concentration of${{\text{H}}^{+}}(\text{aq})$ that is present in the aqueous solution of a given concentration of the acid. Likewise, the strength of a base depends upon the relative concentration of $\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}(\text{aq})$ in an aqueous solution of the base.
  • A neutralisation reaction involves the combination of ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$and $\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}$ions to form water.

${{\text{H}}^{+}}(\text{aq})+\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}(\text{aq})\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$$\quad $

  • By Arthenius concept $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$ should not be a base. It was proposed that in aqueous solution $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$ forms the compound $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{OH}$, which then dissociate as a weak base into $\text{NH}_{4}^{+}$and $\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}$ions

${{\text{H}}^{+}}(\text{aq})+\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}(\text{aq})\xrightarrow[{}]{}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

$\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{O}{{\text{H}}_{(\text{aq})}}\rightleftharpoons \text{NH}_{4}^{+}(\text{aq})+\text{OH}_{(\text{aq})}^{-}$

concepts.title: Bronsted-Lowry concept of acid and bases
concepts.conceptBody:
2. Bronsted-Lowry concept of Acids and Bases

According to this concept, any hydrogen containing species ( a molecule, a cation or an anion), which is capable of donating one or more protons to any other substance, is called an acid .

Any species (molecule, cations or anions) which is capable of accepting one or more protons from an acid, is called a base .

Thus, according to the Bronsted-Lowry concept, an acid is proton-donor, and a base is proton-acceptor.

The reaction of an acid with a base involves transfer of a proton from the acid to the base. So, an acid and a base should be present simultaneously in any system. The extent of an acid-base reaction is governed not only by the proton-donating ability of the acid, but also by the proton-accepting tendency of the base. Acids and bases classified on the basis of this concept are termed as Bronsted acids and bases.

$\text{HC}{{\text{l}}_{(\text{aq})}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}(\text{l})\rightleftharpoons {{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{O}_{(\text{aq})}^{+}+\text{Cl}_{(\text{aq})}^{-}$

In this reaction, $\text{HCl}$ donates its one proton to become $\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}$, and ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$ accepts one proton to become ${{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}}$. Thus, $\text{HCl}$ is Bronsted acid and ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$ is a Bronsted base. For the reverse reaction., ${{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}}$ is able to transfer its proton to $\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}$. So, ${{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}}$is a Bronsted acid and $\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}$is a Bronsted base.

Every acid must form a base on donating its proton, and every base must form an acid on accepting a proton. The base that is produced when an acid donates its proton is called the conjugate base of the acid . The acid that is produced when a base accepts a proton is called the conjugate acid of the base . The above reaction can be written as

$\underset{aci{{d}_{1}}}{\mathop{\text{HCL}}}\,+\underset{\text{bas}{{\text{e}}_{2}}}{\mathop{{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}}}\,\rightleftharpoons \underset{\text{ aci}{{\text{d}}_{\text{2}}}\text{ }}{\mathop{{{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}}}}\,+\underset{\text{ bas}{{\text{e}}_{1}}}{\mathop{\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}}}\,$

In this $\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}$is the conjugate base of the acid $\text{HCl}$ and ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$ is the conjugate base of the acid ${{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}}$. The conjugate acid differs from conjugate base by one proton. A pair of an acid and a base which differ from one another by a proton constitute a conjugate acid base pair. Thus,

Bronsted acid $\xrightarrow{{}}$ Conjugate base of the acid $+{{\text{H}}^{+}}$

Bronsted base $+{{\text{H}}^{+}}\xrightarrow{{}}$ Conjugate acid

Although the Bronsted-Lowry concept of acids and bases is better than the Arrhenius concept, it cannot account for the acidic and basic character of compound not containing hydrogen. For example, acidic nature of oxides such as $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}},\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ etc., and the basic nature of the compounds of the type $\text{CaO},\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{O}$ etc.

concepts.title: Relative strength of conjugate Acid-base pair
concepts.conceptBody:
Relative Strengths of Conjugate Acid-Base Pairs

A stronger Bronsted acid will have a higher tendency for donating proton to the base, hence would tend to exist as its conjugate base. The conjugate base so formed will have very little tendency to pick up a proton, hence would act as a weak base. Thus, there exists an interdependent relationship between the strengths of an acid and its conjugate base.

The stronger an acid, weaker is its conjugate base, and stronger a base, weaker is its conjugate acid.

In water, $\text{HCl}$ acts as a strong acid. As the reverse reaction occurs to a very small extent $\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}$ion acts as a weak base. So, the anion of a strong acid is a weak base. Similarly, the cation of a weak base acts as a strong acid. e.g., $\text{NH}_{4}^{+}$ion in water is a strong acid.

Relative strengths of common conjugate acid-base pairs

Bronsted acid \[\rightleftharpoons \]Conjugate base of the acid + \[{{H}^{+}}\]

\[HCl{{O}_{4}}\]

\[ClO_{4}^{-}\]

\[{{H}_{2}}S{{O}_{4}}\]

\[HSO_{4}^{-}\]

HI

\[{{I}^{-}}\]

HBr

\[B{{r}^{-}}\]

HCl

\[C{{l}^{-}}\]

\[HN{{O}_{3}}\]

\[NO_{3}^{-}\]

\[C{{H}_{3}}COOH\]

\[C{{H}_{3}}CO{{O}^{-}}\]

\[{{H}_{2}}C{{O}_{3}}\]

\[HCO_{3}^{-}\]

\[{{H}_{2}}S\]

\[H{{S}^{-}}\]

\[NH_{4}^{+}\]

\[N{{H}_{3}}\]

HCN

\[C{{N}^{-}}\]

\[{{H}_{2}}O\]

\[O{{H}^{-}}\]

\[N{{H}_{3}}\]

\[NH_{2}^{-}\]

concepts.title: Amphoteric substances
concepts.conceptBody:
Amphoteric substances

Substances which can act as an acid as well as a base are called amphoteric substances . For example, $\text{Al}{{(\text{OH})}_{3}},\text{Zn}{{(\text{OH})}_{2}}$ behave both as acids and as bases in their reactions. The substance, which can donate or accept a proton to act as an acid or a base is termed amphiprotic . For example, the species such as $\text{HS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ and $\text{HC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ are amphiprotic, because of the following reactions.

$\text{HSO}_{4}^{-}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{O}+\text{SO}_{4}^{2-}\text{ }$$\text{( HSO}_{4}^{-}\text{acting as an acid) }$

$\text{HSO}_{4}^{-}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\left( \text{HSO}_{4}^{-}\text{acting as base } \right)$

$\text{HCO}_{3}^{-}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}}+\text{CO}_{3}^{2-}\left( \text{HCO}_{3}^{-}\text{acting as an acid } \right)$

$\text{HCO}_{3}^{-}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\left( \text{HCO}_{3}^{-}\text{acting as an acid } \right)$

Water is the most common solvent showing a unique behaviour. It can act as an acid as well as a base. In reactions like,

$\underset{\text{ acid }}{\mathop{{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}}}\,+\underset{\text{ base }}{\mathop{\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}}}\,\rightleftharpoons \text{NH}_{4}^{+}+\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}$

it acts as an acid, while it behaves like a base in a reaction of the type,

${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}+\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\rightleftharpoons \text{NH}_{4}^{+}+\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}$

The dual role of water molecule may thus be represented by the equation,

$\underset{\text{ aci}{{\text{d}}_{1}}}{\mathop{{{\text{H}}_{2}}O}}\,+\underset{\text{ bas}{{\text{e}}_{2}}}{\mathop{{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}}}\,\rightleftharpoons \underset{\text{ aci}{{\text{d}}_{2}}}{\mathop{{{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}}}}\,+\underset{\text{ bas}{{\text{e}}_{1}}}{\mathop{\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}}}\,$

Water is thus known as an amphiprotic solvent. Solvents which neither donate nor accept protons are called aprotic solvents .

concepts.title: Relative strength of Bronsted acids and bases
concepts.conceptBody:
Relative Strength of Bronsted Acids and Bases

According to Bronsted-Lowry theory, an acid is a proton donor and base is a proton acceptor. Therefore the strength of an acid or a base is determined by its tendency to lose or gain protons. A strong acid is a substance which loses a proton easily to a base.

Relative strength of acids

The relative tendency of acids to transfer a proton to a common base, generally water is expressed as the relative strength of bronsted acids. For example, $\text{HCl}$ has a higher tendency to transfer a proton to ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$ than $\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH}$. This means $\text{HCl}$ is a stronger acid than acetic acid. Thus,

$\text{HCl}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\rightleftharpoons {{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}}+\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}$ (higher tendency for forward reaction)

$\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\rightleftharpoons \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{CO}{{\text{O}}^{-}}+{{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}}$ (small tendency for forward reaction)

Similarly, a base having a higher tendency to accept a proton is stronger. For example, ammonia accepts a proton more readily from water molecule than a water molecule from another water molecule, and hence ammonia is more basic than water.

${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\rightleftharpoons {{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}}+\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}$ (very little tendency for the forward reaction)

${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}+\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\rightleftharpoons \text{NH}_{4}^{+}+\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}$ (little tendency for the forward reaction)

The ability of an acid to lose a proton (acid strength) is described by its acid ionization constant. The larger the value of the acid ionization constant${{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}$, higher is the concentration of ${{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}}$in the solution, stronger is the acid. Knowing the ionization constants of acids one can get the relative strengths of different acids at a particular temperature.

$\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\rightleftharpoons \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{CO}{{\text{O}}^{-}}+{{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}}$

Acetic acid, $\left( \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH} \right){{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}=1.85\times {{10}^{-5}}$

${{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}=\frac{\left[ {{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}} \right]\left[ \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{CO}{{\text{O}}^{-}} \right]}{\left[ \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH} \right]}$

$\text{HF}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\rightleftharpoons {{\text{F}}^{-}}+{{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}}$

Hydrofluoric acid, (HF) $\text{Ka}=6.7\times {{10}^{-4}}\text{Ka}=\frac{\left[ {{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}} \right]\left[ {{\text{F}}^{-}} \right]}{[\text{HF}]}$

Since the ionization constant of $\text{HF}$ is larger than the ionization constant of $\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH}$, hydrofluoric acid (HF) is a stronger acid than acetic acid, $\left( \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH} \right)$.

Relative strength of bases

The ability of a base to accept a proton (basic strength) is described by its base ionization constant $\left( {{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}} \right)$. The larger the value of ${{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}$, higher is the concentration of $\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}$, stronger is the base.

For example, ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}+\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\rightleftharpoons \text{NH}_{4}^{+}+\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}$

${{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}=\frac{\left[ \text{NH}_{4}^{+} \right]\left[ \text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}} \right]}{\left[ \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right]}=1.8\times {{10}^{-5}}$

${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}+{{\text{C}}_{6}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\rightleftharpoons {{\text{C}}_{6}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}\text{NH}_{3}^{+}+\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}\text{, }$

Since the value of ${{\text{K}}_{b}}$ for aniline is much less than that of ammonia, hence aniline $\left( {{\text{C}}_{6}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{2}} \right)$ is a weaker base than ammonia $\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)$.

concepts.title: Lewis concept of Acids and Bases
concepts.conceptBody:
3. LEWIS CONCEPT OF ACIDS AND BASES

In 1938 G.N. Lewis put forward a more generalized acid-base concept.

According to this concept, an acid is a species (charged or uncharged) which, can accept a pair of electrons and a base is a species (charged and uncharged), which can donate a pair of electrons towards the formation of a covalent bond. Thus, a Lewis acid is an electrophilic, and a Lewis base is a nucleophile. For example, in the reaction: $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$ is a donor of electron pair and $\text{B}{{\text{F}}_{3}}$ accepts a pair of electrons. So, according to he Lewis concept,: $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$ is a Lewis base, and $\text{B}{{\text{F}}_{3}}$ is a Lewis acid.

Lewis acids

The following types of compounds or species are Lewis acids:

  • All molecules possessing an atom with incomplete octet of electrons or Lewis acids.

Examples: $\text{B}{{\text{F}}_{3}},\text{AlC}{{\text{l}}_{3}}$

  • All cations are Lewis acids,

Examples: $\text{A}{{\text{g}}^{+}},\text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}},\text{F}{{\text{e}}^{2+}},\text{F}{{\text{e}}^{3+}}$ etc. are Lewis acids.

  • All molecules in which the central atom can acquire more than eight valence electrons.

Examples: $\text{SiC}{{\text{l}}_{4}},\text{Si}{{\text{F}}_{4}}$, etc. are Lewis acids.

  • Molecules with multiple bonds between atoms of different electronegativities e.g. $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}},\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ etc. are Lewis acids.

Lewis bases

The following types of compounds or species are Lewis bases.

  • All anions are Lewis bases e.g. $\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}},\text{C}{{\text{N}}^{-}},\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{CO}{{\text{O}}^{-}}$etc.
  • All molecules having ion-pair of electrons viz: $\text{H}{{\text{N}}_{3}},{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O},\text{CO}$; and amines etc. are Lewis bases. It may be noted that all Bronsted bases are also Lewis bases, but all Bronsted acids are not Lewis acids.

Lewis Acid-Base Reaction

Whenever a Lewis acid reacts with a Lewis base, a covalent bond is formed.

For example, $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ (Lewis acid) reacts with $\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}$(Lewis base) to give $\text{HC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}^{-}$(bicarbonate ion), $\text{B}{{\text{F}}_{3}}$ (boron trifluoride) behaves as a Lewis acid and $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$ (ammonia) as a Lewis base.

Calcium oxide $(\text{CaO})$ behaves as a Lewis base, and sulphur trioxide $\left( \text{S}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)$ as a Lewis acid in the reaction between the two.

The Lewis concept of acids and bases suffers from the following limitations.

  • It fails to account for the relative strength of acids and bases, as it is not based on ionization.
  • An acid-base reaction being an electron-transfer reaction, should be quite fast. However, many Lewis acid-base reactions are slow.
